content.definition: Process of rapid integration and interconnection between countries through foreign trade and foreign investments by multinational corporations (MNCs). It involves the spread of production, services, and technology across borders, connecting distant regions economically.

content.text: Globalisation is not entirely new—trade routes historically connected India to East and West. However, modern globalisation is characterized by: (1) speed—integration occurs rapidly through technology, (2) scale—affects billions of people, (3) depth—involves production networks, not just goods trade. Today's globalisation creates integrated production systems where components are made in different countries, assembled elsewhere, and sold globally.

content.title: Globalisation vs Internationalization
content.text: Globalisation differs from historical internationalization. Past trade involved completed goods moving between countries. Modern globalisation involves production fragmentation—different stages occur in different countries. A shirt might use Korean fiber, be stitched in Bangladesh, marketed from Italy. This complexity creates interdependence between countries.

content.definition: Company that owns or controls production in more than one nation. MNCs establish offices and factories in multiple countries, making them the primary agents of globalisation.

content.text: MNCs set up production in regions where they can get cheap labour and other resources, keeping production costs low and profits high. An industrial equipment MNC designs in the USA, manufactures components in China (cheap labour), assembles in Mexico and Eastern Europe, provides customer service in India, and sells globally. This spread of production across borders is globalisation's defining feature. MNCs have enormous wealth—many exceed developing country governments' budgets—giving them immense power and influence.

MNCs control production through multiple mechanisms: (1) Setting up own factories and offices, (2) Buying local companies and expanding them, (3) Placing orders with small producers globally, determining prices and conditions. The third mechanism is particularly important. Large MNCs in developed countries place orders with small producers in developing countries. Small producers must accept MNC's prices, quality standards, and delivery schedules. MNC determines everything—price, quality, delivery, labour conditions—because it holds the market power.

content.title: Ways MNCs Control Global Production
items: Direct investment: Setting up factories and offices in foreign countries
Acquisition: Buying local companies and integrating them into operations
Outsourcing: Placing orders with small local producers, controlling terms
Joint ventures: Partnering with local companies, often acquiring local technology and networks

content.text: Modern production is fragmented globally. Raw materials come from one country, components from another, assembly in a third, marketing from a fourth, sales from a fifth. This interlinking creates interdependence—disruption anywhere affects the entire chain. Example: A garment MNC gets cotton from India, manufactures in Bangladesh, designs in Italy, markets in USA. If Indian cotton exports are restricted, the entire chain is disrupted.

This global production integration means countries no longer produce complete goods domestically. Instead, countries specialize in specific production stages where they have advantages. India has skilled IT workers; USA has capital and technology; China has cheap labour; Mexico has access to North American markets. Each contributes their advantage, creating efficiency but also vulnerability to global disruptions.

Globalisation integrates markets by removing barriers to trade. Goods produced in one country are easily sold in another. Markets in different countries become interconnected—prices tend to equalize globally, competitors from different countries directly compete. Chinese toys become cheaper in Indian markets, competing with local toy makers. Prices fall for consumers but local producers face losses.

content.title: Chinese Toys Example
content.text: When Chinese toys enter Indian markets at lower prices, Indian toy makers face competition. Within a year, 70-80% of toy shops replace Indian toys with Chinese. Consumers benefit from cheaper prices and variety. Chinese manufacturers expand business. Indian toy makers lose sales and may shut down. This exemplifies market integration's contradictory effects: beneficial for consumers and foreign competitors, harmful for local producers.

Technology is fundamental to globalisation. Transportation improvements reduced shipping costs and time. Container transport revolutionized global trade by reducing port handling costs and speeding delivery. Air transport reduced costs, enabling high-value goods to be shipped globally. Information and communication technology (ICT) is even more revolutionary. Satellites enable instant communication globally. Internet allows instant information access and email communication at negligible cost. Computers enable coordination of complex global production networks. Without these technologies, modern globalisation wouldn't be possible.

content.title: Key Technologies Enabling Globalisation
items: Transportation: Containerization reduced shipping costs; air freight enabled rapid delivery
Telecommunications: Telephone, fax, satellite enabled global communication
Information technology: Internet, email, computers enable instant communication and data transfer
Coordination tools: Software enables managing complex global production networks

content.text: Example: A London magazine designed in Delhi. Text sent via internet to Delhi office. Designers receive design instructions through telecommunications. Designing done on computers. After printing, magazines shipped by air to London. Payment processed through e-banking. The entire process—from design conception to customer delivery—uses technology at every stage, enabling work to happen across continents seamlessly.

content.definition: Process of removing government restrictions on trade and investment. When barriers (taxes on imports, quotas) are removed, businesses can freely import and export goods, and foreign companies can invest freely.

content.text: Historically, many countries protected domestic industries from foreign competition through taxes on imports (tariffs) and import limits (quotas). This protection helped infant industries develop. However, around 1991, India decided this protection was no longer necessary. Government began removing barriers, allowing Indian producers to compete globally and foreign producers to access Indian markets. This liberalisation was supported by powerful international organizations advocating free trade.

content.title: India's Liberalisation in 1991
content.text: Starting 1991, India removed barriers to foreign trade and investment. This policy shift was major—goods could be imported and exported easily; foreign companies could set up factories in India; multinational corporations could operate freely. This liberalisation opened India to globalisation, transforming the economy dramatically.

content.definition: International organization established to liberalize international trade. WTO establishes rules regarding international trade, sees that these rules are obeyed, and pressures countries to remove trade barriers.

content.text: WTO was started at initiative of developed countries and advocates for free trade globally. About 160 countries are WTO members. WTO's rules require countries to remove trade barriers. However, practice reveals inequities: developed countries have retained barriers while forcing developing countries to remove theirs. Developed countries heavily subsidize their agriculture, allowing farmers to sell at artificially low prices, harming farmers in developing countries. Yet WTO pressures developing countries to remove their agricultural support.

Example: US agriculture employs only 0.5% of population but receives massive government subsidies. US farmers can sell at abnormally low prices. These surplus products are sold in developing countries at low prices, undercutting local farmers. Meanwhile, WTO rules force developing countries to remove support to their own farmers. This inequality—developed countries maintaining protections while forcing developing countries to liberalize—is a central critique of WTO.

notes.section_title: 4.5 Impact of Globalisation: Winners and Losers

content.text: Globalisation benefits: (1) Consumers with more choice and lower prices, (2) Well-off sections with purchasing power to access global products, (3) Large Indian companies competing globally, (4) Workers in sectors where India has advantages (IT, software, business services), (5) MNCs earning enormous profits. For example, competition among producers benefits consumers—prices fall, quality improves, variety increases. Urban consumers in India today access products—digital cameras, mobile phones, cars—they couldn't before globalisation.

content.title: Who Benefits from Globalisation
items: Consumers: More choice, lower prices, better quality
Wealthy sections: Access to global luxury goods and services
Large Indian companies: Tata Motors, Infosys, Ranbaxy become multinationals
IT workers: High-wage jobs in software and IT services
MNCs: Enormous profits from operating globally
Urban areas: Better infrastructure, more opportunities

content.text: Globalisation harms: (1) Small producers unable to compete with large foreign competitors, (2) Workers in uncompetitive sectors facing job losses and wage pressure, (3) Rural areas lacking global market access, (4) Vulnerable groups lacking skills for high-wage global jobs. Examples: Indian toy makers lose to Chinese competition; Indian textile workers face lower wages due to competition; small farmers struggle with imported cheap agricultural products.

content.title: Who Loses from Globalisation
items: Small producers: Unable to compete with large MNCs
Workers in declining sectors: Job losses and wage pressure
Rural populations: Limited access to global market opportunities
Vulnerable groups: Landless labourers, artisans lacking relevant skills
Local businesses: Displaced by foreign competitors
Food security: Countries become dependent on food imports

content.title: Ravi's Case: Small Producer's Struggle
content.text: Ravi started a capacitor manufacturing unit in Tamil Nadu. When India removed import restrictions following WTO agreement (2001), foreign capacitors flooded the market at lower prices. His customers (TV manufacturers) started importing capacitors instead of buying from him. By 2005, his production fell from full capacity to less than half. Many workers lost jobs. His story exemplifies how globalisation harms small producers unable to compete with large international competitors.

Globalisation's employment impact is mixed. It creates jobs in some sectors (IT, software, business services, manufacturing for exports) but destroys jobs in others (domestic industries facing import competition). Quality of jobs also varies. IT workers earn high wages with benefits. Garment workers in export industries earn low wages with poor conditions and employment insecurity. Many work as temporary workers without regular employment benefits.

Example: Garment workers in Delhi export industry work for large MNCs but face precarious employment. While globalisation created jobs, employment conditions are poor—low wages, long hours, job insecurity, no benefits. Workers cannot refuse poor conditions because unemployment alternatives are worse. This illustrates globalisation's contradictory effect: creates employment but often in poor-quality jobs.

Globalisation enables development but benefits are concentrated. Those with education, skills, wealth, and market access gain significantly. Those lacking these capabilities—poor, uneducated, in remote areas—gain little or lose. Indian companies (Tata Motors, Infosys, Ranbaxy, Asian Paints) have become global players, creating employment and wealth. But most development benefits concentrate in urban areas among educated workers. Rural areas, particularly those without agricultural exports or industrial development, remain marginalized.

For inclusive development, governments must intervene. Measures include: (1) Protecting workers through labour laws and minimum wages, (2) Supporting small producers with credit, technology, and market access, (3) Negotiating fairly in international organizations like WTO, (4) Ensuring basic services (education, healthcare, infrastructure) reach all, (5) Encouraging technology transfer to developing countries, (6) Building domestic capacity to compete globally. Without these interventions, globalisation reinforces existing inequalities.

content.title: Necessary Interventions for Fair Globalisation
items: Labour protection: Minimum wages, workplace safety, job security regulations
Small producer support: Subsidized credit, technology assistance, market linkages
Fair international negotiations: Pushing back against unequal WTO rules
Infrastructure investment: Roads, electricity, water enabling rural participation
Education expansion: Skills training enabling workers to access better jobs
Social protection: Health insurance, pensions, unemployment benefits

glossary.definition: Process of rapid integration and interconnection between countries through foreign trade, foreign investments by MNCs, and technology. Creates global production and market networks.
Company that owns or controls production facilities in more than one nation. MNCs are primary agents of globalisation, spreading production and investments globally.
Investment by foreign companies in production facilities, technology, and assets in another country. MNCs make FDI when establishing factories or offices.
Exchange of goods and services between countries. Globalisation expands foreign trade by removing barriers and integrating markets.
Fragmentation of production across multiple countries where different stages occur in different locations based on comparative advantages.
Process where markets in different countries become interconnected, enabling goods to flow freely and creating competition across borders.
Removal of government restrictions on trade and investment. Allows free import/export of goods and unrestricted FDI, enabling globalisation.
Government restriction limiting foreign trade, such as tariffs (taxes on imports) or quotas (limits on import quantities).
Tax levied on imported goods, making them more expensive. Used to protect domestic producers from foreign competition.
Limit on quantity of goods that can be imported, restricting foreign goods' market access.
International organization established to liberalize global trade. Establishes rules, monitors compliance, and pressures countries to remove trade barriers.
Concept that countries specialize in production where they have relative cost advantages, enabling efficient global production networks.
Placing goods in containers that can be loaded onto ships, trains, trucks without unloading, reducing port costs and shipping time.
Technology for processing and transmitting information through computers and networks. Enables instant global communication essential for globalisation.
Practice where companies place orders with external suppliers (often in different countries) rather than producing internally, enabling cost reduction and specialization.

summary_points: Globalisation is the rapid integration of countries through MNCs' foreign investments and trade. MNCs fragment production globally—design in one country, manufacture in another, assemble in a third, sell everywhere. This integration creates interdependence but also vulnerability to global disruptions.

Technology (transportation, telecommunications, internet) and liberalisation (removing trade barriers) enable globalisation. WTO pressures countries to liberalize, but unequally—developed countries retain subsidies and protection while forcing developing countries to remove support, creating unfair trading conditions.

Globalisation benefits consumers (more choice, lower prices), wealthy sections, and those with skills/education for global jobs (IT workers earning high wages). It harms small producers unable to compete globally, workers in declining sectors facing job losses and wage pressure, and vulnerable populations lacking market access and skills.

India's development under globalisation is uneven: large Indian companies (Tata Motors, Infosys) become global players; urban areas and educated workers gain opportunities; small producers and rural populations struggle with competition. Employment is created but often in poor-quality jobs (garment workers, casual labourers).

Fair globalisation requires government intervention: protecting workers (labour laws), supporting small producers (credit, technology), negotiating fairly in WTO, expanding education and infrastructure reaching all, and building domestic capacity. Without these, globalisation reinforces inequality rather than enabling inclusive development.
